overallSummary: Advika's learning this week moved across 10 subjects, with a strong emphasis on explaining ideas, applying them in a new situation, and reflecting on how work could improve. The most useful parent role is to ask for evidence and reasoning—not to reteach every lesson or supply the answer.
parentOverview: ManageBac shows the learning opportunities and work recorded for the week; it does not by itself prove mastery. Use this guide to listen for what Advika can explain independently, notice where prompts are needed, and agree on one small next step. A confident explanation, a relevant example, and the ability to correct a mistake are stronger signs of understanding than task completion alone.
strengths: English: Build an engaging narrative opening with a clear character, setting, and problem.
French (i) Phases 1, 2: Introduce self, family, and friends using accurate core vocabulary.
Individuals & Societies: Explain how rights, responsibilities, interdependence, and informed action shape global citizenship.
Sciences: Frame a testable research question and an evidence-based hypothesis.
Mathematics: Move flexibly between fractions, decimals, and percentages.
growthAreas: English: choose the most urgent of 5 pending items, agree on a 20-minute start, and check that submission is complete.
Design: choose the most urgent of 4 pending items, agree on a 20-minute start, and check that submission is complete.
Individuals & Societies: choose the most urgent of 2 pending items, agree on a 20-minute start, and check that submission is complete.
Physical and Health Education: choose the most urgent of 1 pending item, agree on a 20-minute start, and check that submission is complete.

conversationStarters: What is one idea from Myths and legends: A mirror of reality? that you understand differently now?
Show me a piece of work you are proud of. What decision did you make, and why?
Choose one difficult moment from this week. What strategy did you try before asking for help?
Which school idea could help us solve a problem at home or in our community?
coachingTips: Begin with 'Teach me' and wait at least ten seconds; retrieval is more useful than rapid prompting.
Ask for an example, a non-example, or evidence before deciding whether the idea is understood.
Praise the strategy—planning, checking, revising, or persisting—rather than speed or talent.
If the student is stuck, reduce the task to the first visible step and let them choose the next action.

weeklyRoutine.label: Plan · 10 min
weeklyRoutine.action: Choose one pending task and one learning goal; write the first action for each.

weeklyRoutine.label: Explain · 10 min
weeklyRoutine.action: The student teaches one unit without notes; the parent asks one evidence question.

weeklyRoutine.label: Create · 30–45 min
weeklyRoutine.action: Complete one recommended home project with Advika making the decisions.

weeklyRoutine.label: Reflect · 10 min
weeklyRoutine.action: Name one success, one correction, and one specific goal for the next week.

homeProjects.title: Interactive modern myth
homeProjects.purpose: Combine narrative craft with computational thinking by turning a myth into a choice-based PictoBlox story.
steps: Plan a hero, setting, problem, and two meaningful choices on paper.
Storyboard three scenes and decide which event triggers each scene.
Build the story with sprites, dialogue, motion, and at least one branch.
Ask a family member to test it; record one problem and improve it.
homeProjects.parentRole: Be the first user. Ask what each choice reveals about the character instead of suggesting the code or story solution.
reflectionQuestions: Which choice changes the story most?
What did testing reveal that planning did not?

homeProjects.title: Kitchen-table fair test
homeProjects.purpose: Practise scientific investigation and data reasoning with a safe experiment about melting ice.
steps: Choose one factor to change, such as sunlight versus shade.
Write a research question, hypothesis, variables, and fair-test controls.
Measure at equal time intervals and repeat if practical.
Make a graph, write a data-based conclusion, and identify one limitation.
homeProjects.parentRole: Check safety and measurement consistency. Do not rescue an unexpected result—ask what it might mean.
lookFor: A labelled data table or graph
reflectionQuestions: What would make the evidence more reliable?

homeProjects.title: Three-day household plastic audit
homeProjects.purpose: Turn global citizenship into informed local action using evidence rather than assumptions.
homeProjects.estimatedTime: 10 minutes daily for 3 days, then 30 minutes
steps: Define categories such as packaging, bottles, delivery items, and personal care.
Count discarded plastic for three days without changing normal habits.
Graph the categories and identify the largest source.
Propose one realistic seven-day change and explain who must participate.
homeProjects.parentRole: Help collect accurate data and discuss trade-offs such as cost, convenience, hygiene, and access.
lookFor: A claim supported by the audit
reflectionQuestions: Which change would have the greatest realistic impact?

highlights: English: Build an engaging narrative opening with a clear character, setting, and problem.
French (i) Phases 1, 2: Introduce self, family, and friends using accurate core vocabulary.
Individuals & Societies: Explain how rights, responsibilities, interdependence, and informed action shape global citizenship.
Sciences: Frame a testable research question and an evidence-based hypothesis.
Mathematics: Move flexibly between fractions, decimals, and percentages.

subjects.subject: English
subjects.summary: This week connected Myths and legends: A mirror of reality?. Build an engaging narrative opening with a clear character, setting, and problem. Reveal character through actions, dialogue, thoughts, and reactions instead of direct labels. Use myths and legends to explore how beliefs and values shape a story. Classroom evidence included Sample Writing Task and Revision Task.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student can point to a sentence and explain how it creates mood or reveals character.

units.name: Myths and legends: A mirror of reality?
units.summary: The learning focus was to build an engaging narrative opening with a clear character, setting, and problem and to reveal character through actions, dialogue, thoughts, and reactions instead of direct labels.
learningGoals: Build an engaging narrative opening with a clear character, setting, and problem.
Reveal character through actions, dialogue, thoughts, and reactions instead of direct labels.
Use myths and legends to explore how beliefs and values shape a story.
evidenceToListenFor: The student can point to a sentence and explain how it creates mood or reveals character.
The story has a purposeful sequence rather than a list of unrelated events.
commonMisconceptions: Adding more adjectives automatically makes writing vivid.
A myth is only fantasy; strong myths also communicate a culture's values or fears.
parentGuidance: Ask for one sensory detail at a time: what could the character see, hear, or feel?
Let the student revise one paragraph aloud instead of correcting the whole piece for them.
units.homeExtension: Turn a familiar family or community story into a 6-panel modern myth, showing the hero's traits through choices.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “Myths and legends: A mirror of reality?” without looking at your notes.
Read a short section from “Sample Writing Task” and point out one deliberate writing choice and its effect on the reader.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

subjects.subject: French (i) Phases 1, 2
subjects.summary: This week connected Unit 1: Me and Myself. Introduce self, family, and friends using accurate core vocabulary. Build complete sentences with appropriate pronouns, verbs, and simple descriptions. Listen and respond without relying entirely on written prompts.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student can speak for 30–60 seconds with understandable pronunciation.

units.name: Unit 1: Me and Myself
units.summary: The learning focus was to introduce self, family, and friends using accurate core vocabulary and to build complete sentences with appropriate pronouns, verbs, and simple descriptions.
learningGoals: Introduce self, family, and friends using accurate core vocabulary.
Build complete sentences with appropriate pronouns, verbs, and simple descriptions.
Listen and respond without relying entirely on written prompts.
evidenceToListenFor: The student can speak for 30–60 seconds with understandable pronunciation.
The student adapts a sentence to describe a different person rather than memorizing one script.
commonMisconceptions: Translating every English word in the same order produces natural French.
Reading accurately is the same as speaking independently.
parentGuidance: Practise in short daily bursts and respond to meaning before correcting pronunciation.
Use family photos and ask one follow-up question at a time.
units.homeExtension: Create a labelled family gallery and record a one-minute French audio tour introducing three people.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “Unit 1: Me and Myself” without looking at your notes.
Without reading, introduce yourself or a family member in French using complete sentences from “Unit 1: Me and Myself”.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

videos.title: Family Words in French — Learn French With Alexa

subjects.subject: Individuals & Societies
subjects.summary: This week connected What does it Mean to be a Global Citizen?. Explain how rights, responsibilities, interdependence, and informed action shape global citizenship. Consider an issue from more than one stakeholder's perspective. Use evidence to propose a realistic action at local and global scales. Classroom evidence included Homework and Pre-Assessment Task.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student names who is affected and distinguishes an opinion from supporting evidence.

units.name: What does it Mean to be a Global Citizen?
units.summary: The learning focus was to explain how rights, responsibilities, interdependence, and informed action shape global citizenship and to consider an issue from more than one stakeholder's perspective.
learningGoals: Explain how rights, responsibilities, interdependence, and informed action shape global citizenship.
Consider an issue from more than one stakeholder's perspective.
Use evidence to propose a realistic action at local and global scales.
evidenceToListenFor: The student names who is affected and distinguishes an opinion from supporting evidence.
A proposed action is specific, feasible, and linked to the cause of the problem.
commonMisconceptions: Global citizenship only means helping people in other countries.
A large problem has no meaningful local action; small actions matter when they are targeted and sustained.
parentGuidance: Ask whose voice may be missing and what evidence would change the student's view.
Compare one household action with one policy or community action; discuss the value of both.
units.homeExtension: Complete a three-day household plastic audit, graph the categories, and write a short action proposal to reduce the largest source.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “What does it Mean to be a Global Citizen?” without looking at your notes.
Connect “Homework” to a real community issue. Explain who is affected, why it matters, and one responsible action.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

videos.title: What Is Global Citizenship for Kids?

subjects.subject: Sciences
subjects.summary: This week connected LAB SAFETY BASICS, Criterion-C-FA-paper for reference, 1. What do Scientists do?. Frame a testable research question and an evidence-based hypothesis. Identify independent, dependent, and controlled variables for a fair investigation. Present data clearly, interpret patterns, draw a supported conclusion, and suggest improvements. Classroom evidence included LAB SAFETY BASICS and Criterion-C-FA-paper for reference.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student explains what is changed, what is measured, and what must stay the same.

units.name: LAB SAFETY BASICS
units.summary: The learning focus was to frame a testable research question and an evidence-based hypothesis and to identify independent, dependent, and controlled variables for a fair investigation.
learningGoals: Frame a testable research question and an evidence-based hypothesis.
Identify independent, dependent, and controlled variables for a fair investigation.
Present data clearly, interpret patterns, draw a supported conclusion, and suggest improvements.
evidenceToListenFor: The student explains what is changed, what is measured, and what must stay the same.
A conclusion refers to actual data rather than simply saying the hypothesis was correct.
commonMisconceptions: A hypothesis is a guess with no reason; it should make a justified prediction.
One successful result proves a claim; repeated measurements and method quality affect confidence.
parentGuidance: Use everyday scenarios and ask the student to identify variables before discussing answers.
Praise a well-explained limitation or improvement, not only a 'correct' result.
units.homeExtension: Run a fair home investigation comparing how quickly ice melts in two locations; record repeated measurements and graph the results.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “LAB SAFETY BASICS” without looking at your notes.
For “LAB SAFETY BASICS”, give a testable question, hypothesis, variables, fair-test controls, and the evidence you would collect.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

videos.title: Scientific Method for Kids — Learn Bright

One successful result proves a claim; repeated measurements and method quality affect confidence.

Praise a well-explained limitation or improvement, not only a 'correct' result.

subjects.subject: Mathematics
subjects.summary: This week connected SA-1 Practice sheet, Simplifing fractions, Models & Representations: Numbers in Our World. Move flexibly between fractions, decimals, and percentages. Use common denominators and simplify answers accurately. Apply proportional reasoning to practical situations such as discounts, profit, and loss. Classroom evidence included SA-1 Practice sheet and Simplifing fractions.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student explains why a method works instead of only reciting a rule.

units.name: SA-1 Practice sheet
units.summary: The learning focus was to move flexibly between fractions, decimals, and percentages and to use common denominators and simplify answers accurately.
learningGoals: Move flexibly between fractions, decimals, and percentages.
Use common denominators and simplify answers accurately.
Apply proportional reasoning to practical situations such as discounts, profit, and loss.
evidenceToListenFor: The student explains why a method works instead of only reciting a rule.
The student checks the answer and can represent it in a second way.
commonMisconceptions: Adding denominators when adding fractions; equivalent fractions require a common-sized whole.
A calculator result is automatically reasonable; estimation should be used as a check.
parentGuidance: Ask 'How do you know?' and 'Can you show it another way?' before confirming an answer.
Use prices, recipes, or sports statistics so the mathematics has a visible purpose.
units.homeExtension: Plan a ₹1,000 family snack budget with two discounts, compare offers, and justify the best choice using fractions or percentages.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “SA-1 Practice sheet” without looking at your notes.
Create and solve a new problem based on “SA-1 Practice sheet”. Show every step and explain why each step is valid.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

subjects.subject: Guitar
subjects.summary: This week connected Fundamentals of Scales. Recognize and perform steady beat, rhythm patterns, scales, and expressive musical elements. Plan, rehearse, perform, and reflect on a short musical idea. Use tempo, dynamics, and articulation to communicate mood.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student keeps a steady pulse and can restart after an error.

units.name: Fundamentals of Scales
units.summary: The learning focus was to recognize and perform steady beat, rhythm patterns, scales, and expressive musical elements and to plan, rehearse, perform, and reflect on a short musical idea.
learningGoals: Recognize and perform steady beat, rhythm patterns, scales, and expressive musical elements.
Plan, rehearse, perform, and reflect on a short musical idea.
Use tempo, dynamics, and articulation to communicate mood.
evidenceToListenFor: The student keeps a steady pulse and can restart after an error.
The student names one musical choice and explains its effect on the listener.
commonMisconceptions: Playing every note correctly is the only measure of a successful performance.
Faster practice produces faster improvement; slow, accurate repetition is often more effective.
parentGuidance: Ask for a short performance twice and let the student choose one improvement between attempts.
Record the performance so reflection is based on evidence rather than memory.
units.homeExtension: Create and record a 30-second body-percussion or guitar pattern with a clear beginning, variation, and ending.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “Fundamentals of Scales” without looking at your notes.
Use “Fundamentals of Scales” to show how you applied that idea, not only what you completed.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

subjects.subject: Visual Arts
subjects.summary: This week connected IDENTITY THROUGH ART ELEMENTS. Use line, shape, colour, pattern, and composition deliberately to communicate identity. Investigate the origin, materials, symbols, purpose, and cultural significance of an art form. Distinguish respectful inspiration from copying cultural imagery without context. Classroom evidence included Warli Art Form Investigation.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student explains why a visual choice supports the intended meaning.

units.name: IDENTITY THROUGH ART ELEMENTS
units.summary: The learning focus was to use line, shape, colour, pattern, and composition deliberately to communicate identity and to investigate the origin, materials, symbols, purpose, and cultural significance of an art form.
learningGoals: Use line, shape, colour, pattern, and composition deliberately to communicate identity.
Investigate the origin, materials, symbols, purpose, and cultural significance of an art form.
Distinguish respectful inspiration from copying cultural imagery without context.
evidenceToListenFor: The student explains why a visual choice supports the intended meaning.
Research includes cultural context and sources, not only copied images.
commonMisconceptions: Art analysis is just describing what is visible.
Traditional art is decorative only; it can record community, ritual, history, and daily life.
parentGuidance: Ask what the artist wanted the viewer to notice first and how the composition achieves that.
Encourage experimentation with two thumbnail sketches before the final piece.
units.homeExtension: Design a respectful Warli-inspired scene of a present-day family routine, with a short note explaining the symbols and context.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “IDENTITY THROUGH ART ELEMENTS” without looking at your notes.
Use “Warli Art Form Investigation” to show how you applied that idea, not only what you completed.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

videos.title: The Elements of Art — Scratch Garden

subjects.subject: Physical and Health Education
subjects.summary: This week connected Sports Training -Fitness Components. Differentiate health-related and skill-related components of fitness. Connect a training activity to the component it develops. Plan exercise safely using warm-up, technique, recovery, and gradual progression. Classroom evidence included PHE_SA1.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student matches an activity to a fitness component and explains the connection.

units.name: Sports Training -Fitness Components
units.summary: The learning focus was to differentiate health-related and skill-related components of fitness and to connect a training activity to the component it develops.
learningGoals: Differentiate health-related and skill-related components of fitness.
Connect a training activity to the component it develops.
Plan exercise safely using warm-up, technique, recovery, and gradual progression.
evidenceToListenFor: The student matches an activity to a fitness component and explains the connection.
The student proposes a safe, measurable way to improve over time.
commonMisconceptions: Fitness only means endurance or appearance.
More intensity is always better; recovery and correct technique are part of progress.
parentGuidance: Focus discussion on energy, technique, consistency, and wellbeing rather than body shape.
Let the student design a short routine and explain the purpose of each movement.
units.homeExtension: Design and lead a 15-minute family circuit covering three fitness components, then record effort and one improvement.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “Sports Training -Fitness Components” without looking at your notes.
Use “PHE_SA1” to show how you applied that idea, not only what you completed.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.

subjects.subject: Design
subjects.summary: This week connected Unit-1: Designing for Interaction, Staying Safe in the Digital World. Understand sprites, events, motion, looks, sound, control, sensing, and operator blocks. Arrange instructions in a logical sequence to create an interactive response. Research user needs and define success criteria before building a solution. Classroom evidence included Criteria A(iv) and Pictoblox- Blocks categories.
subjects.parentTakeaway: The student can predict what a block sequence will do before running it.

units.name: Unit-1: Designing for Interaction
units.summary: The learning focus was to understand sprites, events, motion, looks, sound, control, sensing, and operator blocks and to arrange instructions in a logical sequence to create an interactive response.
learningGoals: Understand sprites, events, motion, looks, sound, control, sensing, and operator blocks.
Arrange instructions in a logical sequence to create an interactive response.
Research user needs and define success criteria before building a solution.
evidenceToListenFor: The student can predict what a block sequence will do before running it.
The student can explain a bug, change one block, and test whether the change solved it.
commonMisconceptions: A program is correct if it runs once; good design must work reliably for different users.
Research is separate from designing; research should directly shape features and success criteria.
parentGuidance: Ask the student to sketch the algorithm in plain language before opening PictoBlox.
When something fails, ask 'What did you expect? What happened? Which block controls that?'
units.homeExtension: Build a three-question interactive safety quiz in PictoBlox with feedback for correct and incorrect answers.
questions: Teach me the most important idea from “Unit-1: Designing for Interaction” without looking at your notes.
Demonstrate the sequence or design behind “Criteria A(iv)”, then explain how you would test and improve it for a user.
What mistake might someone make in this topic, and how would you correct it?
Where could you use this learning outside school? Give a specific example.
